title: Leaving Christianity
description: A guided pathway for rebuilding biblical identity after leaving Eurocentric doctrine.

lessons.title: Why leaving the lie is only the beginning
lessons.objective: Understand that separation from falsehood must become disciplined obedience.
lessons.body: Leaving inherited lies is necessary, but it is not the finish line. The next work is testing every belief by Scripture, rebuilding habits, and refusing to let reaction become identity.
lessons.reflection: What inherited belief still needs to be tested by Scripture?

lessons.title: Scripture before tradition
lessons.objective: Learn to put Scripture above inherited religious systems.
lessons.body: Tradition must be judged. If a doctrine cannot survive the law, testimony, prophets, and apostolic witness in context, it should not govern the believer.
lessons.reflection: Where have you allowed tradition to define a biblical word?

lessons.title: Covenant identity
lessons.objective: Frame identity through covenant, obedience, scattering, and return.
lessons.body: Biblical identity is not costume, slogan, or argument culture. It is covenant memory, repentance, obedience, and sober recognition of what Scripture says about Israel.
lessons.reflection: How should covenant identity change your daily conduct?

lessons.title: Guardrails: bitterness, pride, isolation
lessons.objective: Identify dangers that can corrupt the return to truth.
lessons.body: Truth without discipline can become pride. Separation without order can become isolation. The return requires humility, correction, patience, and righteous practice.
lessons.reflection: Which guardrail do you need most right now?

title: Who Are the Israelites?
description: A Scripture-first introduction to Israelite identity, covenant, scattering, and restoration.

lessons.title: Israel as a covenant people
lessons.objective: Define Israel by Scripture rather than modern religious assumptions.
lessons.body: Israel is presented as a covenant people called to be holy, distinct, and obedient. Identity and obligation belong together.
lessons.reflection: What does covenant require that ethnicity alone cannot provide?

lessons.title: Scattering and remembrance
lessons.objective: Trace exile, forgetting, and remembrance through biblical prophecy.
lessons.body: Scripture describes scattering and later remembrance. The return begins when the people remember the covenant and test their condition through the Word.
lessons.reflection: How does remembrance differ from internet identity claims?

lessons.title: Prophecy and return
lessons.objective: Connect repentance, restoration, and prophetic hope.
lessons.body: Restoration is not chaos. The prophets connect return with repentance, gathering, obedience, and divine order.
lessons.reflection: Which part of restoration is most often neglected?

lessons.title: Identity without chaos
lessons.objective: Reject unstable identity culture and build disciplined study.
lessons.body: Identity should produce order, clean speech, teachable study, and public credibility. Confusion is not a fruit of truth.
lessons.reflection: What would orderly identity look like in your house?

title: Logic and Scripture
description: Learn how to reason carefully, avoid fallacies, and read Scripture with discipline.

lessons.title: What is logic?
lessons.objective: Understand logic as disciplined reasoning in service of truth.
lessons.body: Logic is not a replacement for Scripture. It is a tool for avoiding contradiction, confusion, and emotional manipulation while handling Scripture carefully.
lessons.reflection: Where have you seen emotion replace reasoning in doctrine debates?

lessons.title: The law of identity
lessons.objective: Learn why a thing must be what it is before it can be interpreted.
lessons.body: Words and claims must be defined. A doctrine that changes definitions mid-argument cannot be trusted.
lessons.reflection: Which biblical term do you need to define more carefully?

lessons.title: The law of non-contradiction
lessons.objective: Recognize why Scripture cannot be interpreted to contradict itself.
lessons.body: A reading that makes one witness destroy another needs correction. Context and precept help keep interpretation coherent.
lessons.reflection: What doctrine have you heard that forces Scripture against itself?

lessons.title: Common fallacies in doctrine debates
lessons.objective: Spot straw man arguments, false dichotomies, and appeals to tradition.
lessons.body: False doctrine often survives by bad reasoning. Learn to identify the claim, assumption, evidence, and conclusion before answering.
lessons.reflection: Which fallacy do you need to stop using yourself?

title: Paul and the Law
description: Correct common Christian misreadings of Paul using context, precepts, and logic.

lessons.title: Paul did not abolish the Law
lessons.objective: Read Paul without making him contradict the prophets or Messiah.
lessons.body: Paul must be read as a Torah-affirming Israelite. Any interpretation that makes him abolish righteousness creates a contradiction with his own statements.
lessons.reflection: What assumption causes people to read Paul against the Law?

lessons.title: Clean and unclean foods
lessons.objective: Separate biblical food law from common proof-texting.
lessons.body: Food law must be read from the Law outward. Passages about people, table fellowship, or man-made traditions should not be forced to erase clean and unclean categories.
lessons.reflection: Which passage is most often misused to erase dietary law?

lessons.title: Faith, works, and obedience
lessons.objective: Avoid the false dichotomy between faith and obedience.
lessons.body: Biblical faith is not rebellion with religious language. Obedience does not purchase God; it expresses covenant loyalty.
lessons.reflection: How can obedience be taught without pride?

lessons.title: Misreading Romans, Galatians, and Colossians
lessons.objective: Learn why context matters in disputed Pauline texts.
lessons.body: Peter warned that Paul could be twisted. Read audience, topic, opponents, and immediate context before building doctrine from isolated lines.
lessons.reflection: What context question should you ask before quoting Paul?

title: Apocrypha Basics
description: Learn how the Apocrypha supports Israelite history, prophecy, exile, and covenant understanding.

lessons.title: Why the Apocrypha matters
lessons.objective: Understand the Apocrypha as historical and instructional study material.
lessons.body: The Apocrypha preserves important wisdom, historical memory, and captivity-era themes. It should be handled responsibly and compared with the broader Scriptural witness.
lessons.reflection: What gap does the Apocrypha help you study?

lessons.title: 2 Esdras and Israel’s condition
lessons.objective: Study lament, judgment, and hope in 2 Esdras.
lessons.body: 2 Esdras gives language for exile, grief, and restoration questions. It must be read with care, humility, and attention to genre.
lessons.reflection: How does exile language clarify the present condition?

lessons.title: Wisdom, discipline, and captivity
lessons.objective: Connect wisdom literature with practical covenant discipline.
lessons.body: Wisdom in the Apocrypha is not abstract. It trains conduct, speech, restraint, family order, and endurance under oppression.
lessons.reflection: Which discipline do you need to practice this week?

lessons.title: Reading the Apocrypha responsibly
lessons.objective: Avoid sensationalism and read with sober method.
lessons.body: Responsible reading tests claims, avoids unstable speculation, and lets Scripture discipline interpretation.
lessons.reflection: What makes an Apocrypha claim responsible or irresponsible?
